county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1884 from Socorro, Dona Ana, and Grant counties. County seat was Hillsboro until 1938 when moved to Hot Springs, renamed Truth or Consequences in 1950. Silver and gold mining district.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sierra County was a major mining district in territorial NM — Hillsboro, Kingston, and Lake Valley silver mining camps drew thousands of prospectors in the 1880s-1890s. County Clerk in Truth or Consequences (formerly Hot Springs) has deeds from 1884. The Geronimo Springs Museum in T or C has local history and genealogy materials. Mining claim records at the County Clerk and BLM are essential. The Black Range mining district produced extensive court records now at SRCA. Pre-1884 records are split among Socorro, Dona Ana, and Grant counties. Catholic and Protestant church records from Hillsboro, Kingston, and surrounding communities. The county was a health resort destination — tuberculosis sanitariums drew patients from across the US whose families settled permanently. Elephant Butte Dam (built 1916) displaced some communities and created new ones.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Truth or Consequences

Wills, estates. Mining-era estates common.

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Sierra County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hillsboro, Kingston, Truth or Consequences, and mining camp cemeteries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Third largest county by area in NM. Socorro settled by Spanish in 1598. Lincoln, Sierra, Catron, and parts of other counties carved out. NM Institute of Mining and Technology (NM Tech) located here.
county_metadata.research_tips: Socorro County has Spanish colonial roots dating to 1598 when Juan de Onate's expedition established a settlement. The county was once enormous, covering much of southern NM before daughter counties were carved out. Catholic parish records from Our Lady of Pillar (Nuestra Senora del Pilar) in Socorro date to the early 1800s and are on FamilySearch. County Clerk in Socorro has deeds from 1852. NM Tech's Martin Speare Memorial Library has regional archives. The Magdalena Livestock Driveway, the last great cattle trail, generated brand records and shipping records. Mining in the Kelly and Magdalena districts produced employment records. SRCA holds territorial court records. The Bosque del Apache National Wildlife Refuge area has historical ranch records. Isleta Pueblo's southern lands extend into northern Socorro County.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial organization.

source_profiles.source_name: Socorro Parish Records — Nuestra Senora del Pilar
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Socorro and surrounding missions.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
Wills, estates from 1852.
Federal census 1850-1950. Originally vast county territory.
Spanish and Mexican land grants in the Socorro area including the Socorro Grant and Bosque del Apache Grant.

county_metadata.record_losses: Taos Pueblo Revolt of 1847 destroyed some early American-era records; Spanish colonial records survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Taos settled since pre-contact (Taos Pueblo is UNESCO World Heritage Site). Spanish settlement from 1615. Important fur trade center. Colfax County carved out 1869. Site of 1847 revolt against US rule.
county_metadata.research_tips: Taos County is one of New Mexico's oldest settled areas, with Taos Pueblo (a UNESCO World Heritage Site) inhabited continuously for over 1,000 years and Spanish settlement dating to 1615. Catholic parish records from Our Lady of Guadalupe in Taos date to the 1700s and are on FamilySearch. The Kit Carson Home and Museum and the Taos Historical Society have manuscript collections. County Clerk in Taos has deeds from 1852. SANM records at SRCA cover colonial-era Taos families extensively. The Taos Pueblo governs its own records; BIA Northern Pueblos Agency records at NARA Denver supplement these. The 1847 Taos Revolt against American occupation generated court records at SRCA. Mountain man era records (Kit Carson, Ceran St. Vrain) are at multiple repositories. The Penitente Brotherhood tradition is strong — referenced in parish records. The Taos art colony (early 1900s) brought Anglo artists whose records are at the Harwood Museum.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial organization. Spanish/Mexican era transfers at SRCA.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Intact from 1852; some pre-US records at SRCA

source_profiles.source_name: Our Lady of Guadalupe & Taos Valley Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Taos, Ranchos de Taos (San Francisco de Asis), Arroyo Seco, Arroyo Hondo, Picuris, and surrounding missions.
Multiple Spanish and Mexican land grants including the Taos Pueblo Grant, Don Fernando de Taos Grant, Arroyo Hondo Grant, and others.

source_profiles.source_name: Taos Pueblo & Picuris Pueblo BIA Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Northern Pueblos Agency
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA census rolls and agency records for Taos and Picuris Pueblos.
Federal census 1850-1950. Spanish colonial padrones at SRCA cover earlier periods.
District court records including 1847 Taos Revolt trials, land grant adjudication, and Kit Carson-era cases.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1903 from parts of Lincoln, Socorro, Valencia, and Bernalillo counties. Named for Francis J. Torrance, NM Central Railway promoter. Estancia is county seat — Estancia Valley homesteading area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Torrance County in the Estancia Valley was one of NM's major homesteading areas in the early 1900s, drawing settlers from across the US via the NM Central Railway. Pre-1903 records are split among Lincoln, Socorro, Valencia, and Bernalillo counties. County Clerk in Estancia has deeds from 1903. The Estancia Valley Historical Society has compiled local family files. Dry farming homesteads had high failure rates — many claims were abandoned, but BLM GLO patent records document original claimants. The Salinas Pueblo Missions National Monument (Quarai, Abo, Gran Quivira) within the county preserves 17th-century Spanish mission history. Catholic parish records from Manzano and surrounding villages predate the county. Pinto bean farming was the primary crop — agricultural records supplement land records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Homesteading-era land transfers.
Catholic parish records from Manzano, Torreon, and surrounding Hispanic villages predating county formation.
Marriage licenses from 1903.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM GLO — Torrance County Homestead Patents
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal homestead patents for the Estancia Valley.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Colfax, Mora, and San Miguel counties. Clayton is county seat — northeastern NM ranching center. Harding County carved out 1921. Named for the Union (US in the Civil War).
county_metadata.research_tips: Union County in NE NM was ranching and homesteading country, where the open range cattle era gave way to homesteading in the early 1900s. Clayton was a shipping point on the Colorado & Southern Railway and site of the infamous hanging of Black Jack Ketchum (1901). County Clerk in Clayton has deeds from 1893; pre-1893 records are in Colfax (Raton), Mora (Mora), and San Miguel (Las Vegas) counties. The Herzstein Memorial Museum in Clayton has local history materials. Brand records at the NM Livestock Board trace ranching families. BLM GLO homestead patents document the wave of settlers from 1900-1920. Catholic parish records from Clayton and Folsom supplement civil records. The Folsom archaeological site in Union County is internationally significant but unrelated to genealogy. Check for Texas and Oklahoma settler origins.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Ranching and homesteading transfers.
Marriage licenses from 1893.
Wills, estates from formation. Ranching estates common.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM GLO — Union County Homestead Patents

Federal census from 1900.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Los Lunas is county seat. Originally vast — McKinley and Cibola counties carved out. Contains Isleta Pueblo. Belen is second-largest city — ATSF railroad center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Valencia County is one of NM's original nine counties, with Hispanic families tracing ancestry to Spanish colonial settlers along the Rio Grande. Los Lunas and Belen have deep genealogical records. Catholic parish records from Our Lady of Belen (Nuestra Senora de Belen, 1793) and Tome parish are on FamilySearch and are primary sources for colonial families. County Clerk in Los Lunas has deeds from 1852. Isleta Pueblo is within county boundaries — tribal enrollment and BIA records at NARA Denver are essential for Isleta families. The Luna-Otero family papers document one of NM's most prominent political dynasties. Belen was a major ATSF railroad junction; railroad employment records trace many families. The Valencia County Historical Society and Harvey House Museum in Belen have local materials. SANM records at SRCA cover colonial Valencia County families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Los Lunas

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial organization.
Catholic parish registers for Belen, Tome, Los Lunas, and surrounding communities. Among oldest parish records in central NM.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Census rolls and agency records for Isleta Pueblo.

Belen Grant, Tome Grant, and other community land grants along the Rio Grande. Court of Private Land Claims adjudication files.
